---
title: "Difference-in-differences for strategic policy evaluation"
description: "Apply difference-in-differences methods to evaluate policy changes in strategic environments. Address parallel trends when agents respond strategically, anticipation effects, and staggered treatment with strategic spillovers."
author: "Raban Heller"
date: 2026-05-08
date-modified: 2026-05-08
categories:
  - causal-inference
  - difference-in-differences
  - policy evaluation
  - strategic behaviour
keywords: ["difference-in-differences", "causal inference", "policy evaluation", "parallel trends", "strategic anticipation", "spillover effects", "synthetic control"]
labels: ["causal-inference", "did", "policy-evaluation"]
tier: 1
bibliography: ../../references.bib
vgwort: "TODO_VGWORT_causal-inference_difference-in-differences-strategic"
image: thumbnail.png
image-alt: "Difference-in-differences plot showing treatment and control group outcomes over time with a policy intervention, strategic anticipation effects visible before treatment, rendered using the Okabe-Ito palette."
citation:
  type: webpage
  url: https://r-heller.github.io/equilibria/tutorials/causal-inference/difference-in-differences-strategic/
license: "CC BY-SA 4.0"
draft: false
has_static_fig: true
has_interactive_fig: true
has_shiny_app: false
---

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(plotly)
okabe_ito <- c("#E69F00", "#56B4E9", "#009E73", "#F0E442",
               "#0072B2", "#D55E00", "#CC79A7", "#999999")
theme_publication <- function(base_size = 12) {
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_size) +
    theme(plot.title = element_text(size = base_size * 1.2, face = "bold"),
          plot.subtitle = element_text(size = base_size * 0.9, color = "grey40"),
          axis.line = element_line(color = "grey30", linewidth = 0.3),
          panel.grid.minor = element_blank(), legend.position = "bottom",
          plot.margin = margin(10, 10, 10, 10))
}
```

## Introduction and motivation

Difference-in-differences (DiD) is one of the most widely used methods for causal inference in observational settings. The design compares the change in outcomes over time between a treatment group (exposed to a policy intervention) and a control group (not exposed), under the assumption that both groups would have followed parallel trends in the absence of treatment. This method has been applied to evaluate minimum wage policies, environmental regulations, healthcare reforms, and countless other interventions. However, a critical challenge arises when the agents affected by the policy are strategic: their anticipatory and adaptive responses can violate the identifying assumptions of the DiD design, leading to biased estimates of the treatment effect.

Strategic behaviour threatens the DiD framework in several distinct ways. First, forward-looking agents may anticipate a policy change and adjust their behaviour before the policy takes effect -- a phenomenon known as anticipation effects. If firms expect a new environmental regulation, they may begin reducing emissions before the regulation is implemented, or alternatively, they may accelerate polluting activities to "stock up" before the constraint binds. Either response creates pre-treatment changes in the treatment group that violate the parallel trends assumption. Standard DiD estimates would then confound the true treatment effect with these anticipatory adjustments, either overstating or understating the policy's causal impact.

Second, strategic spillovers can contaminate the control group. In many policy settings, the treatment and control groups interact through markets, networks, or geographic proximity. When a tax policy change applies to firms in one region, firms in neighbouring regions may adjust their behaviour in response -- for example, by absorbing displaced demand or adjusting prices to compete with treated firms. These general equilibrium effects mean that the control group's outcomes are not truly counterfactual: they reflect indirect effects of the treatment. The stable unit treatment value assumption (SUTVA), which underlies most causal inference methods, is violated when strategic interactions create interdependencies between treated and untreated units.

Third, staggered treatment adoption introduces additional complexity when agents choose when to adopt a policy or enter a market. If the timing of treatment is endogenous -- determined by agents' strategic calculations about the optimal time to comply -- then naive two-way fixed effects (TWFE) regression can produce severely biased estimates. Recent methodological advances by Callaway and Sant'Anna, Sun and Abraham, and others have developed alternative estimators for staggered DiD settings, but these methods still assume that the timing of treatment is independent of potential outcomes, an assumption that strategic behaviour directly threatens.

The game-theoretic perspective provides a structured way to model these threats and develop remedies. By modelling agents as strategic players who optimize over both their actions and the timing of those actions, we can characterize the conditions under which DiD remains valid and design robustness checks that account for strategic responses. For example, if we can model the anticipation structure -- how far in advance agents adjust their behaviour -- we can modify the DiD design to use pre-anticipation periods as the baseline. Similarly, if we can model the network of strategic interactions, we can adjust for spillover effects or identify subsets of control units that are sufficiently isolated from treatment effects.

In this tutorial, we simulate a policy evaluation scenario with strategic anticipation and spillover effects, implement a standard DiD estimator and a corrected estimator that accounts for anticipation, and visualize the bias that strategic behaviour introduces. The simulation demonstrates both the problem and the solution, providing a practical template for applied researchers working in strategic environments.

## Mathematical formulation

Let $Y_{it}$ denote the outcome for unit $i$ at time $t$. Under the standard DiD model:

$$
Y_{it} = \alpha_i + \lambda_t + \tau \cdot D_{it} + \varepsilon_{it}
$$

where $\alpha_i$ are unit fixed effects, $\lambda_t$ are time fixed effects, $D_{it}$ is the treatment indicator, and $\tau$ is the average treatment effect on the treated (ATT).

With strategic anticipation beginning $a$ periods before treatment at $T^*$:

$$
Y_{it} = \alpha_i + \lambda_t + \tau \cdot D_{it} + \sum_{s=1}^{a} \delta_s \cdot \mathbf{1}[t = T^* - s, \, \text{treated}_i] + \varepsilon_{it}
$$

where $\delta_s$ captures the anticipation effect $s$ periods before treatment. Strategic spillovers on control units are modelled as:

$$
Y_{it}^{\text{control}} = \alpha_i + \lambda_t + \phi \cdot \bar{D}_{\mathcal{N}(i),t} + \varepsilon_{it}
$$

where $\bar{D}_{\mathcal{N}(i),t}$ is the fraction of unit $i$'s strategic neighbours that are treated. The naive DiD estimator is:

$$
\hat{\tau}_{\text{naive}} = (\bar{Y}_{\text{treat,post}} - \bar{Y}_{\text{treat,pre}}) - (\bar{Y}_{\text{ctrl,post}} - \bar{Y}_{\text{ctrl,pre}})
$$

This is biased when anticipation or spillovers are present. The bias equals $\delta_{\text{anticipation}} + \phi_{\text{spillover}}$.

## R implementation

```{r}
#| label: compute-did
set.seed(42)

# Simulation parameters
n_treat <- 50
n_control <- 50
n_total <- n_treat + n_control
T_periods <- 20
T_star <- 11  # treatment begins at period 11
anticipation_periods <- 3

# Generate unit fixed effects
alpha_i <- c(rnorm(n_treat, mean = 5, sd = 1),
             rnorm(n_control, mean = 5, sd = 1))

# Time effects (common trend)
lambda_t <- 0.3 * (1:T_periods) + 0.5 * sin(2 * pi * (1:T_periods) / 10)

# True treatment effect
tau_true <- 2.5

# Anticipation effects (strategic agents adjust before treatment)
anticipation_effect <- function(s) {
  0.4 * tau_true * exp(-0.8 * s)  # decaying anticipation
}

# Spillover effect on control group
phi_spillover <- 0.3

# Generate panel data
panel_df <- expand.grid(unit = 1:n_total, time = 1:T_periods) %>%
  mutate(
    treated = unit <= n_treat,
    post = time >= T_star,
    D = treated & post,
    # Base outcome
    Y_base = alpha_i[unit] + lambda_t[time] + rnorm(n(), 0, 0.5),
    # Anticipation effect for treated units
    anticipation = case_when(
      treated & time >= (T_star - anticipation_periods) & time < T_star ~
        anticipation_effect(T_star - time),
      TRUE ~ 0
    ),
    # Spillover effect on control units
    spillover = ifelse(!treated & post, phi_spillover * tau_true, 0),
    # Observed outcome
    Y = Y_base + tau_true * D + anticipation + spillover
  )

# Naive DiD estimate
did_naive <- panel_df %>%
  group_by(treated, post) %>%
  summarise(mean_Y = mean(Y), .groups = "drop") %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = post, values_from = mean_Y, names_prefix = "post_")

tau_naive <- (did_naive$post_TRUE[did_naive$treated == TRUE] -
              did_naive$post_FALSE[did_naive$treated == TRUE]) -
             (did_naive$post_TRUE[did_naive$treated == FALSE] -
              did_naive$post_FALSE[did_naive$treated == FALSE])

# Corrected DiD: exclude anticipation periods from pre-treatment
panel_corrected <- panel_df %>%
  filter(!(treated & time >= (T_star - anticipation_periods) & time < T_star))

did_corrected <- panel_corrected %>%
  group_by(treated, post) %>%
  summarise(mean_Y = mean(Y), .groups = "drop") %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = post, values_from = mean_Y, names_prefix = "post_")

tau_corrected <- (did_corrected$post_TRUE[did_corrected$treated == TRUE] -
                  did_corrected$post_FALSE[did_corrected$treated == TRUE]) -
                 (did_corrected$post_TRUE[did_corrected$treated == FALSE] -
                  did_corrected$post_FALSE[did_corrected$treated == FALSE])

# Spillover-adjusted DiD
tau_spillover_adj <- tau_naive + phi_spillover * tau_true

# Group-time means for plotting
trends_df <- panel_df %>%
  group_by(treated, time) %>%
  summarise(mean_Y = mean(Y), .groups = "drop") %>%
  mutate(group = ifelse(treated, "Treatment", "Control"))

cat(sprintf("True ATT: %.3f\n", tau_true))
cat(sprintf("Naive DiD estimate: %.3f (bias: %.3f)\n", tau_naive, tau_naive - tau_true))
cat(sprintf("Anticipation-corrected DiD: %.3f (bias: %.3f)\n",
            tau_corrected, tau_corrected - tau_true))
cat(sprintf("Spillover-adjusted DiD: %.3f (bias: %.3f)\n",
            tau_spillover_adj, tau_spillover_adj - tau_true))
```

## Static publication-ready figure

```{r}
#| label: fig-did-trends
#| fig-cap: "Difference-in-differences analysis with strategic anticipation effects. The treatment group shows a visible upward shift before the policy intervention at period 11, reflecting forward-looking strategic adjustments. The shaded region marks the anticipation window where strategic agents adjust behaviour before official treatment. The dashed vertical line marks the treatment period, and the counterfactual trend for the treatment group is shown as a dotted line. Rendered using the Okabe-Ito palette."
#| dev: [png, pdf]
#| dpi: 300
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 5

# Construct counterfactual for treatment group
cf_trend <- trends_df %>%
  filter(group == "Treatment", time < (T_star - anticipation_periods))

trend_model <- lm(mean_Y ~ time, data = cf_trend)
cf_all <- data.frame(time = 1:T_periods) %>%
  mutate(mean_Y = predict(trend_model, newdata = .),
         group = "Counterfactual")

p_static <- ggplot(trends_df, aes(x = time, y = mean_Y, color = group)) +
  annotate("rect", xmin = T_star - anticipation_periods, xmax = T_star,
           ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf, fill = okabe_ito[1], alpha = 0.1) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = T_star, linetype = "dashed", color = okabe_ito[8]) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  geom_line(data = cf_all %>% filter(time >= T_star - anticipation_periods),
            aes(x = time, y = mean_Y),
            linetype = "dotted", color = okabe_ito[6], linewidth = 0.8) +
  annotate("text", x = T_star + 0.3, y = max(trends_df$mean_Y) * 0.75,
           label = "Treatment\nbegins", hjust = 0, size = 3, color = okabe_ito[8]) +
  annotate("text", x = T_star - 1.5, y = min(trends_df$mean_Y) + 0.3,
           label = "Anticipation\nwindow", size = 3, color = okabe_ito[1]) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Treatment" = okabe_ito[5],
                                "Control" = okabe_ito[1])) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(2, 20, 2)) +
  labs(title = "DiD with strategic anticipation effects",
       subtitle = sprintf("True ATT = %.1f | Naive DiD = %.2f | Corrected DiD = %.2f",
                          tau_true, tau_naive, tau_corrected),
       x = "Time period", y = "Mean outcome", color = "Group") +
  theme_publication()

print(p_static)
```

## Interactive figure

```{r}
#| label: fig-did-interactive
#| fig-cap: "Interactive DiD plot. Hover over data points to see group means, time periods, and the treatment status for each observation."

p_int <- ggplot(trends_df, aes(x = time, y = mean_Y, color = group,
    text = sprintf("Period: %d\nGroup: %s\nMean outcome: %.3f\nPost-treatment: %s",
                   time, group, mean_Y, ifelse(time >= T_star, "Yes", "No")))) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = T_star, linetype = "dashed", color = okabe_ito[8]) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Treatment" = okabe_ito[5],
                                "Control" = okabe_ito[1])) +
  labs(title = "DiD with strategic anticipation",
       x = "Time period", y = "Mean outcome", color = "Group") +
  theme_publication()

ggplotly(p_int, tooltip = "text") |>
  config(displaylogo = FALSE,
         modeBarButtonsToRemove = c("select2d", "lasso2d"))
```

## Interpretation

The simulation results starkly illustrate how strategic behaviour can distort causal inference when analysts naively apply difference-in-differences without accounting for the strategic environment. The three estimates -- naive DiD, anticipation-corrected DiD, and spillover-adjusted DiD -- each tell a different story, and understanding their divergences is crucial for applied researchers working in strategic settings.

The naive DiD estimator is biased because it conflates two distinct sources of contamination. On the treatment group side, strategic anticipation means that agents in the treatment group begin adjusting their behaviour before the policy officially takes effect. In our simulation, agents adjust up to three periods before treatment, with the anticipation effect decaying exponentially as the distance from the treatment date increases. This pre-treatment shift raises the pre-treatment mean for the treatment group, which reduces the measured pre-post difference and causes the naive DiD to underestimate the true treatment effect. The intuition is straightforward: if agents have already partially adjusted before the policy, the measured change at the time of implementation captures only the residual adjustment, not the full treatment effect.

On the control group side, strategic spillovers contaminate the counterfactual. When treated firms change their behaviour -- say, by reducing output in response to a new regulation -- untreated competitors may expand to fill the gap. This raises the post-treatment outcomes for the control group, inflating the pre-post difference for controls and further biasing the DiD estimate. The direction of this bias depends on the sign of the spillover: positive spillovers (where treatment benefits the control group) cause downward bias in the DiD estimate, while negative spillovers cause upward bias. In our simulation, the spillover is positive, contributing to the underestimation of the treatment effect.

The anticipation-corrected estimator addresses the first source of bias by excluding the anticipation window from the pre-treatment period. By using only the pre-anticipation observations as the baseline, this estimator recovers a pre-treatment mean that is uncontaminated by strategic adjustment. The improvement is substantial in our simulation, reducing the bias from the naive estimate considerably. However, this correction requires the analyst to know (or estimate) the length of the anticipation window, which in practice depends on how far in advance agents learn about the policy and how quickly they can adjust. Event study designs, which estimate treatment effects separately for each lead and lag period, provide a diagnostic tool for detecting anticipation: statistically significant pre-treatment coefficients suggest that agents are adjusting before the policy takes effect.

The spillover adjustment addresses the second source of bias but requires knowledge of the spillover parameter. In practice, this parameter must be estimated from auxiliary data or from structural models of the strategic environment. Alternatively, researchers can seek control groups that are geographically or economically isolated from the treatment group, reducing the scope for strategic spillovers. Synthetic control methods offer another approach: by constructing a synthetic control unit as a weighted combination of untreated units, the analyst can potentially dilute spillover effects, although this is not guaranteed if all potential controls are affected.

The broader lesson is that causal inference and game theory are not separate enterprises -- they are deeply intertwined whenever the agents whose behaviour we study are strategic. The parallel trends assumption, the cornerstone of DiD identification, is a statement about counterfactual outcomes that can be violated by strategic behaviour in predictable ways. By modelling the strategic environment explicitly, researchers can anticipate these violations, design appropriate corrections, and produce more credible causal estimates. The combination of game-theoretic modelling and causal inference methods represents a frontier in applied microeconomics that promises to improve policy evaluation in settings where strategic behaviour is the norm rather than the exception.

## Extensions and related tutorials

- [Algorithmic fairness as a multi-stakeholder game](../../ethics-applications/algorithmic-fairness-games/) -- evaluating fairness interventions using causal methods.
- [Bayesian games and incomplete information](../../bayesian-methods/bayesian-games-incomplete-information/) -- modelling strategic anticipation through Bayesian belief updating.
- [Prospect theory and reference-dependent preferences in games](../../behavioral-economics/prospect-theory-reference-dependence/) -- how behavioural biases affect strategic responses to policy changes.
- [Regression discontinuity in strategic settings](../../causal-inference/regression-discontinuity-strategic/) -- an alternative causal design for strategic environments.
- [Synthetic control methods](../../causal-inference/synthetic-control/) -- constructing counterfactuals when spillovers contaminate natural control groups.

## References

::: {#refs}
:::
